name: Proxemic Transitions (Shape-Changing Furniture)
short_description: A CHI 2017 study of actuated shape-changing furniture that reconfigures between tabletop and wall-display arrangements to mediate the proxemics of informal meetings.
long_description: Proxemic Transitions investigates how furniture-scale shape-changing interfaces can physically reconfigure to support collaboration, gradually transitioning between tabletop and vertical wall-display layouts to reshape how people stand, sit, and orient toward one another in informal workplace meetings. Grounded in proxemics theory and empirical studies, the authors articulate three design aspects: transition speed, stepwise reconfiguration, and radical shifts. As physical AI / interaction design, it matters because it treats the built environment itself as an active, motorized participant whose changing form steers human spatial behavior, rather than treating intelligence as something hidden in a screen.
ai_note: The system is an actuated, human-controlled shape-changing furniture prototype with interactive surfaces; no AI/ML drives its behavior.

images.caption: Shape-changing furniture prototype transitioning between tabletop and wall-display configurations to mediate meeting proxemics.
images.alt_text: A motorized interactive furniture surface shown reconfiguring from a flat tabletop into an upright wall-like display around which people gather.

sources.title: Proxemic Transitions: Designing Shape-Changing Furniture for Informal Meetings
Proxemic Transitions: Designing Shape-Changing Furniture for Informal Meetings (Aarhus University record)

interactions.title: Flatten the table to gather close, raise a wall to step back
interactions.description: A small group leans in around a low horizontal tabletop for hands-on work; when they want to present, the surface tilts and rises into a near-vertical wall display, pushing people upright and back into a standing audience arrangement.

interactions.title: Pace the room by how fast the furniture moves
interactions.description: The authors treat transition speed as a design knob: a slow creep lets people drift and rearrange themselves comfortably, while a brisk shift commands attention and signals a change of activity, so the furniture sets the social tempo of the meeting.

interactions.title: Step through intermediate layouts instead of all-or-nothing
interactions.description: Through stepwise reconfiguration the surface pauses at in-between angles and heights, letting a group settle into a half-raised, half-flat arrangement that suits a hybrid sit-and-discuss moment rather than snapping straight to a finished shape.

interactions.title: Trigger a radical shift to reset the gathering
interactions.description: A radical shift dramatically re-forms the furniture between fundamentally different configurations, physically breaking up one spatial formation and herding people into a new one to mark the boundary between, say, brainstorming and review.

interactions.title: Balance physical motion against on-screen change
interactions.description: Grounded in proxemics theory and field studies of informal meetings, the design steers when to move the physical form versus when to transform the digital content on its interactive surface, so the room's geometry and the shared display shift in concert.

interactions.title: Carried forward into the KirigamiTable tabletop
interactions.description: The same group reworked these ideas into KirigamiTable (CHI 2020), an actuated shape-changing tabletop that physically deforms its surface to support proxemic transitions in collaborative work, testing cooperative control and how people anticipate the shape change.

interactions.title: Furniture that opens a circle for a newcomer
interactions.description: When someone new approaches a huddle, the table could ease its edge down and widen its footprint to physically make room, using a slow stepwise transition to invite them in without anyone having to scoot their chair.

interactions.title: Standing-meeting timer you can feel
interactions.description: For a time-boxed stand-up, the surface starts as a tall, lean-against wall and gradually lowers toward a sit-down tabletop as the clock runs out, letting the room read the remaining time from the furniture's posture instead of a screen.
